active_answer: The reverse crossing hitch will hold tension once tied; it is not as solid as other locking hitches but also remains easy to untie
active_question: What tradeoff does Rope365 give for the reverse crossing hitch?
edited_answer: It holds tension once tied and remains easy to untie, but Rope365 says it is less solid than other locking hitches.
edited_question: What tradeoff does Rope365 give for the reverse crossing hitch?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement preserves all three source-stated properties in a complete, explicitly attributed comparison.
reason: The replacement preserves all three source-stated properties in a complete, explicitly attributed comparison.
support_evidence: The reverse crossing hitch (also called reverse Munter hitch) has the same topology as the crossing hitch but is tied in the opposite direction. It’s nice to know both when you want to create symmetry, but they also function differently. The reverse crossing hitch will hold tension once tied; it is not as solid as other locking hitches but also remains easy to untie.

active_answer: two ropes of the same size, or a smaller bight to a larger rope’s running ends
active_question: When does Rope365 say a sheet bend is appropriate for joining two ropes?

external_verification.authority: Connecticut Fire Academy Recruit Firefighter Program
external_verification.claim: For different diameters, place the bight in the larger rope and use the smaller rope's working end.
Make the bight in the thicker rope and pass the thinner rope's end through and around it.

reason: The source places the bight in the smaller rope and the running end in the larger rope. Authoritative rescue references specify the opposite for unequal diameters: form the bight in the larger or thicker rope and tie with the smaller or thinner rope's working end.
support_evidence: Instead of using the square knot, I use a sheet bend to join my ropes. The beauty of the sheet bend is that it draws it’s strength from a bowline structure, so it is strong, but also easy to untie even after being put under heavy load. In plain english, you can use a sheet bend to join two ropes of the same size, or a smaller bight to a larger rope’s running ends. Do not use it to join a bigger rope to a smaller rope’s running ends or the join will usually fail.

active_answer: the forces inside the rope will make the rope shift in different ways
active_question: Why can crossing-hitch direction matter even when differences seem minimal at 90 degrees on a flat plane?
edited_answer: Rope bondage is three-dimensional, so aiming the rope left, right, up, or down changes its internal forces and can make it shift in different ways.
edited_question: Why can crossing-hitch direction matter even when differences seem minimal at 90 degrees on a flat plane?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement connects the source fragment to its three-dimensional premise and lists only the directional variables named in the evidence.
reason: The replacement connects the source fragment to its three-dimensional premise and lists only the directional variables named in the evidence.
support_evidence: The crossing hitch is asymmetric in two directions, it can be tied from the left or right, and the top and bottom. The difference between these will be minimal if the ropes cross at 90 degrees on a flat plane. But rope bondage works in three dimensions, as we aim slightly left or right, up or down, the forces inside the rope will make the rope shift in different ways.
